items.title: Hailing India as a ‘natural partner,’ Carney says Canada can secure a free trade deal by year’s end
items.summary_auto: Prime Minister Mark Carney’s Indian charm offensive continued over the weekend as he set aside any notion of bilateral bad blood and hailed the South Asian country as a "natural partner."

items.title: Alberta’s reliance on oil revenues means that when prices fall, the economy follows
items.summary_auto: The Alberta government's new budget projects years of deficits due to slumping oil prices. Some experts say the province’s predictions for price recovery may be optimistic.

items.title: The rise of singlehood could impact everything from housing to taxes
items.summary_auto: For decades there has been an increase in single people across the world. But more recently that number seems to be not only growing, but changing how we talk about single people and how the world might work with more of them.

items.title: Canada poised to become 'one of the largest suppliers of LNG in the world': Energy minister
items.summary_auto: Canada's energy minister says the country is poised to become one of the world's biggest suppliers of liquefied natural gas, exporting as much as 100 million tonnes per year.

items.title: Canada should not have an election before reviewing CUSMA trade deal, says Poilievre
items.summary_auto: Conservative Leader Pierre Polievre says Canada should not have an election before it reviews the Canada-U.S.-Mexico Agreement (CUSMA) this year — and instead he's reiterating his proposal the Carney government set up an all-party committee on the matter.

items.title: Why much of Canada's national art collection isn't online
items.summary_auto: Canada has the technology to share its national art collection online. What it hasn’t clearly decided is how much copyright law should stand in the way.

items.title: Snowbanks are still blocking parking on some streets more than a month after Toronto's last big storm
items.summary_auto: More than a month after Toronto's last major snowstorm, some residents are complaining their streets are still so clogged with curbside snowbanks that finding on-street parking remains a problem — not just for residents but local businesses too.
